graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to outline the *complete* legal framework (including the full list of permitted uses/exceptions, all pre-deployment safeguards/limitations, and all notification/reporting duties). The excerpts available cover only a limited set of rules and principles, mainly around prohibition/strict exceptions, authorisation, and interaction with data-protection law.

Based strictly on the context provided, the framework elements are:

- Permitted uses / general rule
  - Use of AI systems for “real-time” remote biometric identification of natural persons in publicly accessible spaces **for the purpose of law enforcement** is described as **particularly intrusive** and is subject to a **prohibition with strict, targeted exceptions** (“prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric identification”; “targeted exceptions” taking account of law enforcement needs). The context does not list the specific exception categories.

- Pre-deployment safeguards and limitations
  - Any use should be **limited by strict necessity** (“should be limited by strict necessity”).
  - The use is framed as requiring it to be **legitimate, proportionate, and strictly necessary** (these characteristics are explicitly tied to post-remote systems in the context, but the “strict necessity” limitation is explicitly stated for real-time systems).
  - The Regulation is **not intended to be the legal basis** for the underlying processing of personal data under **Article 8 of Directive (EU) 2016/680**; processing must comply with applicable Union/national data-protection rules (including the Charter and secondary Union law).

- Authorization requirements and competent authority
  - **Each use case** of a real-time remote biometric identification system in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement should be subject to an **express and specific authorisation**.
  - The authorisation must be granted by a **judicial authority** or an **independent administrative authority of a Member State**, and the decision must be **binding**.
  - Authorisation should **in principle be obtained prior to use**, with a view to identifying a person/persons.
  - The “real-time remote biometric identification system” is also described as **requiring authorisation** from those authorities.

- Notification and reporting obligations
  - Member States may adopt national rules on the “possibility” to authorise such use and related detailed rules; **such national rules should be notified to the Commission within 30 days of adoption**.
  - The context also indicates a broader “notification obligation” and that “national competent authorities” send notifications to the Commission, but it does not provide the detailed content (what must be reported, when, and by whom in operational deployments).

If you want, paste the relevant article text/sections (or additional pages) covering the exceptions/derogations and operational reporting duties, and I can produce a “complete” structured outline that matches the Regulation precisely.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Outline the complete legal framework for law enforcement use of real-time remote biometric identification: permitted uses, pre-deployment safeguards and limitations, authorization requirements and authority, and notification and reporting obligations.

Such national rules should be notified to the Commission within 30 days of their  adoption. (38) The use of AI systems for real-time remote biometric identification of natural persons in publicly accessible spaces  for the purpose of law enforcement necessarily

  case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement to lead  to indiscriminate surveillance. The conditions for post-remote biometric identification should in any case not  provide a basis to circumvent the conditions of the prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric  identification. (35) Each use of a ‘real-time’ remote biometric identification system in publicly accessible spaces for the purpose of law  enforcement should be subject to an express and specific authorisation by a judicial authority or by an independent  administrative authority of a Member State whose decision is binding. Such authorisation should, in principle, be  obtained prior to the use of the AI system with a view to identifying a person or persons.

chunks.text: detailed rules of national law. Consequently, Member States remain free under this Regulation not to provide for such a possibility at all or to only provide for such a possibility in respect of some of the objectives capable of justifying authorised use identified in this Regulation. Such national rules should be notified to the Commission within 30 days of their adoption. (38) The use of AI systems for real-time remote biometric identification of natural persons in publicly accessible spaces for the purpose of law enforcement necessarily

case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement to lead to indiscriminate surveillance. The conditions for post-remote biometric identification should in any case not provide a basis to circumvent the conditions of the prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric identification. (96) In order to efficiently ensure that fundamental rights are protected, deployers of high-risk AI systems that are bodies governed by public law, or private entities providing

each use case in each of the situations mentioned above. (35) Each use of a ‘real-time’ remote biometric identification system in publicly accessible spaces for the purpose of law enforcement should be subject to an express and specific authorisation by a judicial authority or by an independent administrative authority of a Member State whose decision is binding. Such authorisation should, in principle, be obtained prior to the use of the AI system with a view to identifying a person or persons.

and process such data in connection thereto on the grounds listed in Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680. In that context, this Regulation is not intended to provide the legal basis for the processing of personal data under Article 8 of Directive (EU) 2016/680. However, the use of real-time remote biometric identification systems in publicly accessible spaces for purposes other than law enforcement, including by competent authorities, should not be covered by the specific framework regarding such use for
